heroOverview: Hematometra is the accumulation of blood within the uterine cavity resulting from an obstruction that prevents normal menstrual outflow. The obstructing lesion may be congenital — an imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum, or cervical atresia — or acquired, developing after cervical stenosis from a prior procedure, intrauterine adhesions, or a uterine or cervical tumour. Cyclical menstrual blood that cannot escape progressively distends the uterine cavity, producing a painless enlarged uterus or, more typically, severe cyclical pain with primary or secondary amenorrhea. The diagnosis is made on pelvic ultrasound, which shows an anechoic or echogenic fluid-filled endometrial cavity. Relief of the obstruction — by dilating the cervix, excising a membrane, or lysing adhesions — allows drainage and resolves the distension. Without treatment, hematometra can progress to bilateral hematosalpinx, endometrial infection, and permanent infertility.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact population prevalence is uncertain; imperforate hymen, the most common congenital cause, occurs in approximately 1 in 1,000 female births; cervical stenosis after uterine procedures is estimated to affect 1–4% of women undergoing loop electrosurgical excision or cryotherapy of the cervix
keyStats.demographics: Congenital causes present at menarche (ages 11–14); acquired causes peak in reproductive and perimenopausal years
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital hematometra: ages 11–16 at presentation; acquired hematometra: ages 25–60 depending on aetiology
keyStats.globalCases: Cervical stenosis and intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome) collectively represent the most common acquired substrate; Asherman syndrome follows curettage procedures in an estimated 1–30% of cases depending on procedure type

definition: Hematometra (ICD-10: N85.7) is defined as the pathological distension of the uterine cavity by retained blood, arising from any condition that creates a functional or anatomical obstruction to normal menstrual egress. The uterine body is normally a muscular hollow organ lined by the endometrium, which sheds cyclically under hormonal control; if the cervical canal or lower genital tract is occluded, the shed endometrium and blood have no exit route and accumulate within the cavity. The resulting haematometra can be acute — rapid filling over one or a few cycles — or chronic, slowly enlarging over months to years when the obstruction is partial. Congenital causes include imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum, cervical atresia, and vaginal agenesis; these present most dramatically at menarche. Acquired causes include post-procedural cervical stenosis after loop electrosurgical excision procedure (LEEP), cryotherapy, cone biopsy, or endometrial ablation, as well as intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome), endometrial or cervical cancer causing luminal obstruction, and radiation-induced fibrosis of the cervix. The condition is managed by gynaecologists and, when caused by structural anomalies, by paediatric or adolescent gynaecologists. Secondary involvement of the fallopian tubes produces concurrent hematosalpinx if the obstruction is longstanding, further compromising fertility and increasing the risk of pelvic infection.

typesClassification.description: Caused by a developmental anomaly obstructing the lower genital tract: imperforate hymen (most common), transverse vaginal septum, cervical atresia, or vaginal agenesis. Presents at or shortly after menarche with primary amenorrhea and cyclical lower abdominal pain.

Cervical stenosis developing after LEEP, cryotherapy, cone biopsy, dilation and curettage, or endometrial ablation. The treated cervix scars and narrows, obstructing menstrual outflow over weeks to months after the procedure.

Intrauterine adhesions following endometrial curettage, myomectomy, or infection obliterate part or all of the uterine cavity. Blood accumulates in the portion of the cavity that remains open, behind the adhesion band, producing a localised or complete haematometra.

An endometrial, cervical, or vaginal tumour — including carcinoma — can physically obstruct the cervical canal, causing blood to accumulate proximal to the lesion. This is the most urgent to exclude, particularly in postmenopausal women with a blood-filled uterus.

primarySymptoms: Cyclical lower abdominal or pelvic pain that recurs monthly at the expected time of menstruation but without visible blood loss — the pathognomonic symptom of complete outflow obstruction in a menstruating woman.
Primary amenorrhea in adolescents with a congenital obstructive anomaly — no menstruation has ever occurred despite normal pubertal development including breast growth and pubic hair.
Secondary amenorrhea in women who previously menstruated normally but develop acquired cervical stenosis or intrauterine adhesions that obstruct drainage.
A midline pelvic or suprapubic mass corresponding to the distended blood-filled uterus, which may be palpable on abdominal examination when haematometra is large.
A bulging blue-tinged or tense membrane at the vaginal introitus in adolescents with imperforate hymen, formed by the distended vaginal vault and haematocolpos below the obstruction.
Lower back pain and urinary symptoms including urinary retention or frequency when a large haematometra compresses the bladder base or ureters.
Nausea and malaise accompanying the cyclical pelvic pain, reflecting prostaglandin release from the obstructed and distended uterine cavity.
Postmenopausal uterine distension with vaginal discharge or bleeding in older women, where cervical stenosis from prior procedures or malignancy obstructs the natural drainage of uterine secretions.
Infertility or implantation failure, particularly in women with Asherman syndrome, where intrauterine adhesions prevent normal embryo implantation as well as blood drainage.
Fever, purulent vaginal discharge, and acute pelvic pain when secondary infection of the blood-filled uterine cavity produces pyometra — a serious complication requiring urgent drainage.
earlyWarningSigns: Cyclical pelvic cramping without menstrual flow in a girl or woman who would otherwise be expected to menstruate.
Progressive lower abdominal fullness and discomfort building over several months in a teenager with delayed menarche.
Gradual cessation of menstrual flow following a uterine or cervical procedure, replaced by worsening dysmenorrhea.
A pelvic mass detected on routine examination in an adolescent with no prior gynaecological assessment.
emergencySigns: Fever, rigors, foul-smelling vaginal discharge, and acute uterine tenderness indicating pyometra — secondary infection of the haematometra — which requires urgent hospital admission, intravenous antibiotics, and emergency drainage.
A tense, painful, rapidly enlarging abdominal mass in an adolescent with complete outflow obstruction, creating risk of tubal rupture and peritonitis from bilateral haematosalpinx.
Urinary retention caused by compression of the bladder base or urethra by a massively distended haematometra or haematocolpos, requiring catheterisation alongside uterine drainage.
Signs of septic shock — high fever, hypotension, confusion, tachycardia — in a woman with a known haematometra who has not been drained.

causes.description: The hymen fails to perforate during foetal development, leaving a complete membrane obstructing the vaginal introitus. Cyclic menstrual blood accumulates first in the vagina (haematocolpos) and then, as pressure increases, fills the uterus. Imperforate hymen occurs in approximately 1 in 1,000 girls and is the most common cause of haematometra in adolescents at menarche.

causes.cause: Cervical stenosis after uterine or cervical procedures
causes.description: LEEP, cone biopsy, cryotherapy, endometrial ablation, and D&C can cause reactive cervical scarring that progressively narrows the internal os over weeks to months. Tightly stenosed internal os obstructs menstrual outflow; the risk is highest after multiple or deep cervical procedures and is estimated at 1–4% after LEEP.

Adhesions between the anterior and posterior endometrial walls form after curettage — particularly when performed after a pregnancy loss or for postpartum haemorrhage — or after severe endometritis. Thick adhesions in the uterine cavity or at the internal os obstruct blood drainage from segments of the uterus above the adhesion band, causing compartmental or complete haematometra.

Transverse vaginal septa, cervical atresia, and partial vaginal agenesis (Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome variants) create partial or complete lower genital tract obstruction. These anomalies are identified on pelvic MRI and require surgical correction by specialist gynaecologists with paediatric or reconstructive surgical expertise.

causes.cause: Cervical or uterine malignancy causing luminal obstruction
causes.description: A cervical carcinoma, endometrial carcinoma, or large intracavitary fibroid polyp can physically obstruct the cervical canal, causing menstrual blood and secretions to pool above the obstruction. In postmenopausal women, blood pooling in the uterus without prior procedure history raises the index of suspicion for malignancy and mandates urgent evaluation.

Pelvic radiotherapy for cervical, endometrial, or rectal cancer causes progressive fibrosis of the cervical stroma, gradually obliterating the canal. Radiation-induced cervical stenosis producing haematometra can develop months to years after treatment completion, particularly when the cervix is directly included in the radiation field.

riskFactors.description: A history of LEEP, cone biopsy, cryotherapy, endometrial ablation, or dilatation and curettage increases the risk of cervical stenosis and subsequent haematometra; the risk is highest after multiple procedures or deep tissue excisions.

riskFactors.factor: Prior uterine curettage after pregnancy loss
riskFactors.description: Curettage following miscarriage or postpartum haemorrhage is the dominant cause of Asherman syndrome-related haematometra; approximately 15–30% of women who undergo multiple curettage procedures develop intrauterine adhesions.

Girls with imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum, or cervical atresia will inevitably develop haematometra at menarche if the obstruction is not diagnosed and corrected; family history of Mullerian anomalies increases suspicion.

Women treated with pelvic or cervical radiation for gynaecological or colorectal cancer are at risk of progressive cervical fibrosis; annual imaging surveillance can detect early haematometra before it becomes symptomatic or infected.
Any tumour obstructing the cervical canal should be excluded in a woman — especially postmenopausal — presenting with a fluid-filled uterine cavity on imaging.
Ascending infection producing endometritis can cause intrauterine adhesion formation that secondarily obstructs drainage; prompt antibiotic treatment of PID reduces this risk.

Women who have had both endometrial ablation and prior tubal ligation can develop cyclical haematometra in residual uterine horns that continue to produce menstrual blood but cannot drain; this is a recognised late complication of combined procedures.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents at menarche (ages 11–16) with congenital outflow tract obstruction, presenting with primary amenorrhea and cyclical pain
Reproductive-aged women following uterine or cervical procedures who develop secondary amenorrhea and dysmenorrhea
Postmenopausal women with cervical stenosis or a uterine tumour causing blood or secretion accumulation
Women with Asherman syndrome seeking fertility treatment in whom haematometra is a concurrent finding
Survivors of pelvic malignancy who received radiotherapy and develop progressive cervical fibrosis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with the clinical history, which typically reveals cyclical pelvic pain with absent menstrual flow in an adolescent or, in acquired cases, secondary amenorrhea and worsening dysmenorrhea after a uterine procedure. Pelvic examination findings depend on the level of obstruction: a bulging bluish membrane at the introitus indicates haematocolpos from imperforate hymen; a visibly distended uterus on abdominal examination suggests substantial haematometra. Transvaginal or transabdominal ultrasound is the primary investigation and characteristically shows a distended uterine cavity filled with anechoic fluid (fresh blood) or heterogeneous echogenic material (older blood products). Concomitant tubal distension on ultrasound suggests haematosalpinx from retrograde blood flow. MRI of the pelvis provides comprehensive anatomy in complex congenital anomalies and accurately identifies the level and nature of the obstruction, the uterine anatomy, and any associated renal or other Mullerian abnormalities. Serum beta-hCG is checked to exclude pregnancy in any reproductive-aged woman. In women with postmenopausal haematometra, office hysteroscopy or pipelle endometrial biopsy following cervical dilation is mandatory to exclude endometrial carcinoma, which can produce cervical obstruction as part of its natural history. Cervical cytology and colposcopy are performed when cervical stenosis follows prior treatment for CIN or when a cervical cancer cannot be excluded.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary imaging modality: identifies a fluid-filled or echogenic distended uterine cavity and quantifies the degree of haematometra; detects co-existing haematosalpinx or adnexal involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is gently inserted into the vagina (transvaginal) or placed on the lower abdomen (transabdominal) for 10–15 minutes. Transabdominal is preferred in adolescents who have not had penetrative intercourse. No radiation is involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the complete anatomy of Mullerian duct anomalies, confirms the level and type of obstruction, assesses uterine and renal anatomy, and characterises the nature and age of blood products; essential for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in an MRI scanner for 30–45 minutes performing breath-hold sequences. No ionising radiation. A gadolinium contrast injection is given in most adults for tissue characterisation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory in postmenopausal women or those with risk factors for endometrial carcinoma to exclude malignancy as the cause of outflow obstruction before dilating the cervix.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After dilating the cervical os, a thin flexible tube is gently passed into the uterine cavity to suction a small tissue sample. This takes 1–2 minutes in clinic and causes brief uterine cramping.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes pregnancy as a cause of uterine distension or pelvic mass in any reproductive-aged woman before proceeding to instrumental investigation or surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; results available within 30–60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the uterine cavity to assess the extent and nature of intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome), confirm the level of obstruction, and guide simultaneous adhesiolysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin lighted scope is passed through the cervix into the uterine cavity under sedation or general anaesthesia. The procedure takes 15–30 minutes and causes post-procedure cramping for 1–2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for cervical malignancy when stenosis follows prior CIN treatment or when cervical cancer cannot be excluded as the obstructing lesion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A speculum examination followed by application of acetic acid and examination of the cervix under a magnifying colposcope; takes 10–15 minutes and causes minimal discomfort.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of haematometra is directed at relieving the underlying obstruction to allow blood to drain and to prevent recurrence. The urgency of intervention depends on whether infection is present — pyometra is an emergency — and on the degree of uterine distension and associated pain. For congenital obstructions such as imperforate hymen, surgical incision of the hymeneal membrane under brief general anaesthesia allows immediate drainage of haematocolpos and haematometra; most girls resume normal menstrual function within a few cycles. Transverse vaginal septa require more complex resection and vaginal reconstruction, ideally by a surgeon with paediatric gynaecology or reconstructive experience. Acquired cervical stenosis is treated in clinic or under anaesthesia by progressive cervical dilation using Hegar dilators, which opens the canal, allows blood to drain, and can be followed by insertion of a dilator or Foley catheter to maintain patency during healing. For Asherman syndrome with associated haematometra, hysteroscopic adhesiolysis under general anaesthesia divides the adhesion bands under direct vision; hormonal therapy with high-dose oestrogen is prescribed after lysis to promote re-epithelialisation and reduce re-adhesion. Malignancy causing cervical obstruction is managed according to its stage and type, with drainage providing palliation while definitive oncological treatment proceeds. Where recurrent cervical stenosis is the problem, regular cervical dilation at intervals of 4–8 weeks may be required in the long term, and balloon dilatation under fluoroscopic guidance has been used in refractory cases.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose oestrogen therapy after adhesiolysis
medicalTreatments.description: Following hysteroscopic lysis of intrauterine adhesions, sequential high-dose oestrogen (typically oral conjugated oestrogen 1.25–2.5 mg or oral oestradiol 2–4 mg daily for 30–60 days) promotes rapid endometrial regeneration and re-epithelialisation of the raw surfaces exposed by adhesiolysis, reducing the risk of re-adhesion. Progesterone is added in the second half of the cycle to induce a withdrawal bleed and maintain a patent cavity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces re-adhesion rates by approximately 50–60% compared with adhesiolysis alone in controlled studies; intrauterine pregnancy rates of 30–60% after treatment of moderate-to-severe Asherman syndrome.

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics and NSAIDs
medicalTreatments.description: Paracetamol and NSAIDs (ibuprofen, mefenamic acid) manage the cyclical pelvic pain from haematometra during the diagnostic and pre-treatment period. NSAIDs also reduce uterine cramping from prostaglandin release. They do not address the obstruction and are a bridging measure while awaiting definitive treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide meaningful pain relief in approximately 70% of women with dysmenorrhea from outflow obstruction but do not alter the underlying condition.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for pyometra
medicalTreatments.description: When haematometra is complicated by secondary bacterial infection (pyometra), broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering aerobic and anaerobic organisms — typically a beta-lactam combined with metronidazole — are given before and during cervical drainage. Antibiotics alone without drainage are insufficient; drainage is mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IV antibiotics plus drainage achieve clinical resolution of pyometra in over 90% of cases; mortality from pyometra in treated patients is below 1% in modern series.

medicalTreatments.name: Misoprostol for cervical priming
medicalTreatments.description: Vaginal or sublingual misoprostol (400–800 micrograms) softens and dilates the cervix, facilitating subsequent mechanical cervical dilation or hysteroscopic entry in women with tight stenosis. Administered 6–12 hours before the procedure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces failed cervical entry at operative hysteroscopy from approximately 5–8% to less than 2% in women with cervical stenosis in controlled trials.

surgicalOptions.name: Cervical dilation (Hegar dilators)
surgicalOptions.description: Progressive insertion of graduated metal dilators through the cervical os under local anaesthesia or sedation physically opens the stenotic canal, allowing haematometra to drain. A Foley catheter balloon or silicone stent may be left in place for 2–4 weeks to maintain patency during healing. Can be repeated as an outpatient procedure in recurrent stenosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective drainage achieved in over 95% of cases of simple cervical stenosis; recurrence requiring repeat dilation occurs in approximately 20–40% of post-procedural stenosis cases within 12 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical incision of imperforate hymen (hymenotomy)
surgicalOptions.description: A cruciate or elliptical incision through the imperforate membrane under brief general anaesthesia allows immediate drainage of haematocolpos and haematometra. The margins are trimmed to prevent closure and the vaginal mucosa edges are sutured to maintain patency. The procedure takes 15–30 minutes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Nearly 100% anatomical success; uterine function, menstrual regularity, and fertility are preserved in the vast majority of adolescents when performed promptly.

surgicalOptions.name: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis for Asherman syndrome
surgicalOptions.description: Under direct hysteroscopic visualisation, intrauterine adhesions are divided with scissors, an electrosurgical loop, or a laser, restoring uterine cavity anatomy. The extent of lysis is guided by the preoperative adhesion grade (American Society for Reproductive Medicine classification); severe disease may require multiple staged procedures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restoration of normal uterine cavity in approximately 70–80% of mild-to-moderate Asherman syndrome; live birth rates after treatment are approximately 30–60% depending on initial adhesion severity.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical correction of vaginal septum or cervical atresia
surgicalOptions.description: A transverse vaginal septum is excised and the vaginal ends are anastomosed; cervical atresia may require canalization of the cervical remnant or, in complete atresia, hysterectomy as a last resort. These complex reconstructions are performed by subspecialty centres with paediatric and adolescent gynaecological expertise.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional outcome depends on septum thickness and location; low septa have success rates over 90%; high septa and cervical atresia carry higher rates of re-stenosis and fertility compromise.

alternativeTreatments.name: Balloon dilatation under fluoroscopic guidance
alternativeTreatments.description: For refractory cervical stenosis where repeated mechanical dilation fails, a small balloon catheter is guided into the stenotic cervical canal under fluoroscopic imaging and inflated to progressively widen the canal. Used in post-radiation stenosis where tissue is friable and direct instrumentation risks perforation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) intrauterine instillation
alternativeTreatments.description: An emerging adjunctive treatment after hysteroscopic adhesiolysis, PRP derived from the patient's own blood is instilled into the uterine cavity to promote endometrial regeneration. Early small studies suggest improved endometrial thickness and pregnancy rates, but evidence is insufficient for routine guideline endorsement.

whySeeSpecialist: Any adolescent presenting with primary amenorrhea and cyclical pelvic pain should be referred urgently to a gynaecologist, since untreated congenital haematometra causes progressive tubal and ovarian damage. A gynaecologist should also be consulted for any woman with secondary amenorrhea and dysmenorrhea after a uterine procedure, for postmenopausal women with a fluid-filled uterine cavity on imaging — where malignancy must be excluded — and for any woman suspected to have pyometra, which requires same-day admission and drainage. Reproductive endocrinologists manage the fertility implications of Asherman syndrome and complex congenital anomalies.
doctorSelectionGuide: For congenital haematometra in adolescents, seek a gynaecologist with paediatric and adolescent gynaecological subspecialty training and access to paediatric anaesthesia for hymenotomy or septum resection. For acquired haematometra and Asherman syndrome, a reproductive endocrinologist with high-volume hysteroscopic adhesiolysis experience provides the best fertility outcomes. For postmenopausal haematometra, a general gynaecologist or gynae-oncologist can perform biopsy and drainage concurrently while coordinating malignancy workup. Ask about the surgeon's annual hysteroscopy and adhesiolysis volume and their approach to post-lysis oestrogen therapy.
preventionStrategies: Ensure thorough and anatomically precise repair of perineal and cervical tears at delivery to prevent post-partum cervical stenosis.
Avoid unnecessary or repeated uterine curettage; where curettage is required after pregnancy loss, minimally traumatic technique and concurrent antibiotic prophylaxis reduce adhesion formation.
Perform routine cervical examination 6–8 weeks after cervical procedures (LEEP, cone biopsy) to detect early stenosis before it becomes complete.
Prescribe serial gentle cervical dilation prophylactically after endometrial ablation in women with prior tubal ligation to prevent post-ablation syndrome.
Screen girls at menarche for symptoms of outflow obstruction: any girl with cyclical pain but no menstrual flow should have a pelvic ultrasound and hymeneal inspection.
lifestyleModifications: Attend all scheduled follow-up appointments after cervical or uterine procedures so that early stenosis can be detected and managed before haematometra develops.
Report any change in menstrual pattern — cessation of flow, worsening pain, or increasing pelvic heaviness — promptly rather than tolerating it as normal variation.
Keep a menstrual diary noting pain severity and flow volume; this assists clinical assessment of post-procedural stenosis.
In adolescents: understand that the primary amenorrhea and monthly pain from an undiagnosed obstructive anomaly will worsen with each menstrual cycle until drained, making timely reporting essential.
After adhesiolysis, maintain the prescribed oestrogen course consistently for the full duration advised, as incomplete cycles allow early re-adhesion.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory dietary patterns (Mediterranean diet: olive oil, fish, vegetables, legumes) to reduce prostaglandin-mediated cyclical pelvic pain
High-fibre foods and adequate hydration to maintain bowel regularity and reduce constipation-related pelvic pressure
Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, lentils, spinach, fortified cereals) if chronic blood loss or nutritional deficiency has occurred from prolonged haematometra drainage after treatment
avoid: NSAIDs during early post-hysteroscopy recovery if prescribed oestrogen therapy, as the combination may increase the risk of endometrial mucosal irritation
Excessive caffeine, which can aggravate cyclical pelvic pain through prostaglandin pathways

exerciseGuidelines: During acute haematometra with significant pelvic pain, rest and avoidance of high-impact activity are appropriate. After drainage — whether by hymenotomy, cervical dilation, or hysteroscopy — light activity can resume within 1–5 days. Full exercise is typically permitted within 1–2 weeks after simple drainage procedures and within 4–6 weeks after complex reconstructive surgery. Swimming and water immersion are avoided for at least 4 weeks after vaginal surgery to reduce infection risk. Regular moderate aerobic exercise once treated is encouraged and may reduce prostaglandin-mediated menstrual pain on an ongoing basis.
dailyManagement: Complete the full prescribed oestrogen and progesterone course after adhesiolysis without interruption.
Attend all post-operative hysteroscopy checks — typically at 4–8 weeks — to assess cavity patency and detect early re-adhesion.
Report immediately if fever, purulent discharge, or worsening pelvic pain develops after drainage, as these suggest pyometra or wound infection.
Keep a menstrual calendar to track the return of regular flow after treatment, which confirms sustained patency.

Discuss the timing of pregnancy attempts openly with the managing gynaecologist — spontaneous conception should be deferred by at least 3 months after complex hysteroscopic adhesiolysis to allow endometrial recovery.
prognosis: The prognosis of haematometra is closely tied to the speed of diagnosis and completeness of the obstructing cause removal. Adolescents with imperforate hymen treated at menarche by hymenotomy have excellent long-term prognosis: uterine and tubal function is fully preserved in the great majority when drainage is achieved before recurrent tubal obstruction causes permanent damage. Similarly, post-procedural cervical stenosis detected early and dilated promptly carries an excellent prognosis with restoration of normal menstrual flow. The most guarded prognosis applies to severe Asherman syndrome with pan-uterine adhesions: even after skilled hysteroscopic adhesiolysis, normal cavity restoration is achieved in only approximately 60–70% of severe cases, and live birth rates remain around 30–50% despite optimal treatment. Malignancy-related haematometra has a prognosis determined by the tumour stage and histology. When secondary pyometra complicates any form of haematometra, prompt drainage and antibiotics are curative in over 90% of cases when instituted before septic shock develops.
recoveryTimeline: After hymenotomy for imperforate hymen, most girls return to school and normal activity within 3–7 days; menstruation normalises within 1–3 months. After cervical dilation for acquired stenosis, drainage is immediate and cramping settles within 2–3 days; a dilator or Foley catheter may remain in place for 2–4 weeks. After hysteroscopic adhesiolysis, light activity resumes in 2–3 days; the full oestrogen-progesterone course runs 6–8 weeks. Uterine cavity re-assessment by hysteroscopy or saline infusion sonography is performed at 4–8 weeks post-operatively. For complex vaginal septum resection, recovery takes 4–8 weeks and includes vaginal dilator therapy to prevent re-stenosis.
complications: Pyometra — secondary bacterial infection of the haematometra producing an acutely ill patient with fever, purulent discharge, and uterine tenderness; requires urgent drainage and intravenous antibiotics.
Hematosalpinx — retrograde blood flow from a longstanding haematometra distends one or both fallopian tubes, potentially causing permanent tubal mucosal damage and compromising fertility.

Endometriosis — retrograde menstruation from outflow obstruction seeds endometrial cells onto pelvic peritoneum; women diagnosed late with congenital haematometra have a higher rate of endometriosis at laparoscopy.
Uterine perforation during cervical dilation — over-forceful instrumentation of a tightly stenosed or softened uterus risks perforation, requiring laparoscopic assessment.
Re-adhesion after adhesiolysis — intrauterine adhesions recur in approximately 20–40% of moderate-to-severe Asherman syndrome cases despite optimal post-operative hormonal therapy.
Permanent infertility — when haematometra from any cause is left untreated until both tubes are irreversibly damaged and the endometrium is scarred, natural conception becomes impossible.

supportResources.description: NHS patient guide to intrauterine adhesions, a leading acquired cause of haematometra, including symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment.

supportResources.name: ACOG patient FAQ — Abnormal uterine bleeding
supportResources.description: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists patient education on uterine bleeding disorders and outflow obstruction.

supportResources.name: Ectopic Pregnancy Trust — related conditions
supportResources.description: Information on tubal and uterine conditions including those that can coexist with or follow on from haematometra and haematosalpinx.

sources.title: March CM. Management of Asherman syndrome. Reprod Biomed Online, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Intrauterine synechiae (Asherman syndrome)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Intrauterine synechiae (Asherman syndrome) are the adhesions themselves; haematometra is the blood-filled uterine cavity that results when those adhesions or any other obstruction prevents menstrual drainage. The two diagnoses are closely linked but represent the cause versus the consequence.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hematosalpinx
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hematosalpinx is blood pooling in the fallopian tube; haematometra is blood pooling in the uterine cavity. They are distinguished by ultrasound, which localises the fluid to the tube (tubular sausage-shaped) versus the uterine body (distended endometrial cavity). The two can coexist when uterine outflow obstruction is longstanding.

confusedWithConditions.name: Secondary amenorrhea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Secondary amenorrhea is the absence of menstruation for 3 or more months in a woman who previously menstruated; haematometra is one cause of secondary amenorrhea. Other causes — hypothalamic amenorrhea, hyperprolactinaemia, premature ovarian insufficiency — produce amenorrhea without blood accumulation in the uterus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary amenorrhea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary amenorrhea has many causes including gonadal dysgenesis and hypothalamic causes that do not produce haematometra; haematometra-related primary amenorrhea specifically results from outflow obstruction with normal ovarian function and blood accumulation behind the barrier.

relatedConditions.name: Hematosalpinx
relatedConditions.relevance: Longstanding haematometra causes retrograde blood flow into the fallopian tubes, producing concurrent haematosalpinx; prompt drainage of the uterus prevents progressive tubal damage.

relatedConditions.name: Intrauterine synechiae
relatedConditions.relevance: Asherman syndrome — intrauterine adhesions — is the most common acquired cause of haematometra; hysteroscopic lysis of these adhesions is the cornerstone of surgical treatment for both conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Primary amenorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Haematometra from congenital outflow obstruction is a structural cause of primary amenorrhea that must be excluded when a girl presents without menstruation at expected menarche.

relatedConditions.name: Secondary amenorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Acquired cervical stenosis causing haematometra is a structural cause of secondary amenorrhea; excluding blood accumulation on ultrasound is part of the work-up for any woman with secondary amenorrhea after uterine procedure.

faqs.question: What is hematometra?
faqs.answer: Hematometra is a condition in which blood accumulates inside the uterine cavity because an obstruction prevents normal menstrual outflow. The obstruction can be congenital — such as an imperforate hymen — or acquired after a uterine procedure, intrauterine scarring, or a tumour blocking the cervical canal.

faqs.question: What causes hematometra?
faqs.answer: The most common congenital cause is imperforate hymen, which presents at menarche. Acquired causes include cervical stenosis after LEEP, cone biopsy, or endometrial ablation; intrauterine adhesions from prior curettage (Asherman syndrome); and cervical or endometrial tumours obstructing the canal. Post-radiation cervical fibrosis is a cause in cancer survivors.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hematometra?
faqs.answer: The hallmark symptom is cyclical lower abdominal pain at the expected time of menstruation with absent or minimal menstrual flow. Adolescents may have primary amenorrhea with monthly cramps. Acquired cases present with secondary amenorrhea and worsening dysmenorrhea. A palpable lower abdominal mass develops when the uterus is significantly distended.

faqs.question: Is hematometra dangerous?
faqs.answer: Untreated hematometra can lead to serious complications. The most urgent is pyometra — secondary infection of the blood-filled uterus producing fever, sepsis, and acute pelvic pain that requires emergency drainage. Prolonged obstruction can also distend and damage the fallopian tubes, causing permanent infertility.

faqs.question: How is hematometra diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made on pelvic ultrasound, which shows a distended uterine cavity filled with fluid or echogenic blood products. Pelvic MRI provides detailed anatomy for congenital anomalies and surgical planning. Endometrial biopsy is mandatory in postmenopausal women to exclude malignancy as the cause of obstruction.

faqs.question: How is hematometra treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment relieves the obstruction. Imperforate hymen is corrected by hymenotomy — a small incision under brief anaesthesia. Cervical stenosis is treated by progressive cervical dilation. Intrauterine adhesions causing haematometra are divided by hysteroscopic adhesiolysis. In all cases, post-procedural hormonal therapy promotes healing and prevents recurrence.

faqs.question: Can hematometra cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Blood accumulation distends and damages the fallopian tubes if obstruction is prolonged, and endometrial scarring (Asherman syndrome) impairs implantation. Fertility is well preserved when congenital obstruction is corrected early at menarche. Asherman syndrome-related haematometra carries live birth rates of 30–60% after hysteroscopic adhesiolysis depending on severity.

faqs.question: What is pyometra and how is it related to hematometra?
faqs.answer: Pyometra is a blood-filled uterus that has become secondarily infected with bacteria, producing pus rather than blood. It is a serious complication of untreated haematometra. Symptoms are fever, foul-smelling vaginal discharge, and acute pelvic pain. Treatment is urgent hospital admission with intravenous antibiotics and immediate cervical drainage.

faqs.question: Can hematometra occur after endometrial ablation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Endometrial ablation destroys the uterine lining but may leave residual functioning endometrium in the uterine horns. If the cervical canal then scars, blood produced by this residual tissue cannot drain, forming a haematometra. This risk is higher in women who also have had tubal ligation — called post-ablation tubal sterilisation syndrome.

faqs.question: What is the recovery like after hematometra treatment?
faqs.answer: After simple cervical dilation or hymenotomy, recovery is rapid: cramping settles within 2–3 days and normal activity resumes within a week. After hysteroscopic adhesiolysis for Asherman syndrome, light activity resumes in 2–3 days and a 6–8 week hormonal course follows. Full hysteroscopic re-assessment is performed at 4–8 weeks.

faqs.question: How is imperforate hymen related to hematometra?
faqs.answer: An imperforate hymen is the most common congenital cause. The unperforated membrane blocks all vaginal outflow. At menarche, blood fills the vagina (haematocolpos) and then, as pressure increases, overflows into the uterus causing haematometra. A bluish bulging membrane at the introitus is the classic physical finding, and hymenotomy is curative.

faqs.question: Can hematometra develop after LEEP or cone biopsy?
faqs.answer: Yes. LEEP, cone biopsy, and cryotherapy can cause reactive cervical scarring that progressively narrows the internal os over weeks to months. This obstruction accumulates menstrual blood in the uterus. Women who develop secondary amenorrhea with dysmenorrhea after a cervical procedure should be evaluated for cervical stenosis and haematometra by ultrasound.

faqs.question: What is Asherman syndrome and how does it cause hematometra?
faqs.answer: Asherman syndrome is the formation of intrauterine adhesions, most commonly after uterine curettage following pregnancy loss. These adhesion bands can obstruct blood drainage from the uterine cavity above them, producing a compartmental or complete haematometra. Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis restores cavity anatomy and resolves the obstruction.

faqs.question: Does hematometra go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No. The obstructing lesion does not resolve spontaneously. Each menstrual cycle adds more blood to the already full cavity, progressively distending the uterus and increasing the risk of tubal damage and infection. All symptomatic haematometra requires surgical or procedural drainage and correction of the underlying obstruction.

faqs.question: What imaging is best for hematometra?
faqs.answer: Transvaginal or transabdominal ultrasound is the first-line test and shows the blood-filled distended uterine cavity. Pelvic MRI provides detailed anatomy for congenital anomalies, confirms the type and level of obstruction, and identifies associated renal anomalies. MRI is essential for planning corrective surgery in complex congenital cases.

faqs.question: Can hematometra be confused with a uterine fibroid?
faqs.answer: On clinical examination a distended haematometra and a large fibroid can both produce a midline pelvic mass. Ultrasound distinguishes them readily: haematometra appears as an anechoic or echogenic fluid-filled endometrial cavity, while fibroids are solid hypoechoic intramural or submucosal masses. An endometrial biopsy may be needed to exclude concurrent endometrial pathology.

faqs.question: Can postmenopausal women get hematometra?
faqs.answer: Yes. In postmenopausal women, cervical stenosis from age-related fibrosis, prior procedures, or radiation obstructs the drainage of normal uterine secretions, producing a fluid- or blood-filled uterus on ultrasound. Any postmenopausal haematometra requires urgent evaluation to exclude endometrial or cervical malignancy as the underlying cause of obstruction.

faqs.question: Will hematometra treatment restore my fertility?
faqs.answer: Fertility outcomes depend on the cause. Congenital obstruction corrected early carries an excellent fertility prognosis. Mild-to-moderate Asherman syndrome achieves live birth rates of approximately 50–60% after hysteroscopic lysis. Severe pan-uterine adhesions have lower success rates of 30–40% even with optimal treatment; IVF or gestational surrogacy may be considered in these cases.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of pyometra complicating hematometra?
faqs.answer: Warning signs are fever above 38°C, foul-smelling or purulent vaginal discharge, sudden worsening of pelvic pain, abdominal tenderness, and feeling systemically unwell. These signs in a woman known to have haematometra require same-day emergency assessment, IV antibiotics, and urgent cervical drainage.

faqs.question: Can hematometra recur after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cervical stenosis recurs after dilation in approximately 20–40% of cases within 12 months and may require repeat dilation or long-term stenting. Intrauterine adhesions recur in 20–40% of moderate-to-severe Asherman cases after hysteroscopic lysis. Consistent post-operative hormonal therapy and follow-up hysteroscopy reduce but do not eliminate recurrence risk.

metaDescription: Hematometra is blood trapped in the uterus from outflow obstruction. Learn causes including imperforate hymen, cervical stenosis, and treatment options.
